EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad shortwave crossing southern Canada pushed an associated cold front into southern Canada during the early afternoon. A pre-frontal trough moved into eastern New York during the early afternoon with individual cells and line segments forming into one large squall line with bowing segments. The squall line pushed into central and southern new hampshire by mid afternoon and reached the coast by early evening. Good heating ahead of this line with dew points in the upper 60s to lower 70s resulted in capes of 1500 to 3000 j/kg across much of the region. Although shear was limited, stronger cells moving around 35 kts transported strong mid level winds to the surface resulting in numerous reports of wind damage. Stronger cells also produced quarter to golf ball size hail. One long lived cell in Kennebec County produced hail at least 3 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed multiple trees and branches near Kennebunk.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was toppled on to a driveway by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving surface boundary which produced thunderstorms over western most New Hampshire and Vermont on the 15th...slowly advected into western Maine. With ample surface heating and increasing dew point values (over Maine)...Cape's increased into the 1500-2000 j/kg range. LI's dropped to around -6. Low freezing levels allowed for storm's to produce hail. Wind fields aloft were modest.| |Sea breeze also played a role as it progressed inland all the way to Paris, ME and intersected with several other lingering boundaries visible on radar. Dewpoints were very low, in the upper 40s, with temperatures reaching the upper 80s. Behind the sea breeze, dewpoints rose into the upper 50s and low 60s. Flow aloft was about 20KT out of the NNW, and when combined with a SSE flow behind the sea breeze, this was able to yield enough shear for one significant supercell over Kennebec County. Low level dry air (inverted V sounding) indicated a wind threat, but did not materialize. Also, slow moving thunderstorms posed a minor flooding threat as some areas may have received up to 2 inches of rain in a very short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Rome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across Aroostook County along a nearly stationary frontal boundary. The storms developed during the late afternoon and continued into the evening. The storms primarily produced damaging winds...though two weak tornadoes also occurred. Heavy rain from training thunderstorms produced localized flash flooding in the Fort Kent area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced 2.00 to 4.00 inches of rain in a short time producing flash flooding. The flash flooding damaged or destroyed several culverts on the North Perley Brook Road...with portions of the road also washed out.

A strong shortwave digging into northern New England produced plunging mid and upper level temperatures resulting in steep lapse rates across the forecast area. Freezing levels dropping to around ten thousand feet set the stage for large hail with some of the stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near New Sharon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave digging into northern New England produced plunging mid and upper level temperatures resulting in steep lapse rates across the forecast area. Freezing levels dropping to around ten thousand feet set the stage for large hail with some of the stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.25 inch hail in Monmouth.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced numerous reports of 1.25 inch hail in Richmond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with a pre-frontal trough moved through the forecast area during the afternoon and early evening of July 4th. Ahead of this boundary was very unstable air with high surface dew points and large CAPE values of 2000-2500 J/kg. Wind flow was relatively weak with limited wind shear. These storms produced many reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.75  inch hail near the general store in Cambridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Irene made landfall across western Long Island, NY and was downgraded to a Tropical Storm as it moved into and through New England.  The center of Irene was located just to the southwest of New Hampshire at 5 pm Sunday evening August 28 and then travelled up the Connecticut River Valley to the northern border of New Hampshire by 11 pm.  The storm brought a prolonged period of strong and gusty winds and heavy rain to Maine.  The high winds snapped or uprooted numerous trees throughout the state causing more than 300,000 customers to lose electrical and/or communication services.  The heavy rains caused rivers and streams in Western Maine to flood causing damage to bridges, roads, and property.||The strongest winds across the state began Sunday morning in southern areas and spread northward during the day.  The strong winds continued into the evening as the center of Irene moved through the Connecticut River Valley.  Winds continued to be gusty overnight as the storm moved away from the area. Observed maximum wind gusts included 56 mph at Augusta, 52 mph at Portland, 45 mph at Lewiston, and 44 mph at Fryeburg.  The combination of wet soil and the prolonged period of strong and gusty winds brought down numerous trees throughout the state.||Rainfall amounts across the state ranged from 1 to 3 inches along the coast to 3 to 7 inches across most of the remainder of western Maine with the heaviest precipitation falling in the mountains.  Rainfall was elevation dependant with the highest elevations receiving the greatest amounts.  In the mountains, much of the rain fell within a period of only several hours causing serious flash flooding of rivers and streams across Oxford, Franklin, and Somerset Counties.  Several bridges and roads were washed out or damaged by flash floods.  In addition, runoff from the heavy rains caused damage to nearby counties in western Maine.  Observed precipitation amounts included between 6 and 7 inches at Phillips and Andover, and between 5 and 6 inches at Temple, Kingfield, Sumner, and Hartford.   The Wild River at Gilead had a record crest of 17.42 feet with a corresponding flow of 36,848 cubic feet per second. ||Along the coast, the combination of high tides and large waves caused minor flooding, beach erosion, and splashover in the more vulnerable areas.

These storms produced many reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a large oak on cars near North Windham.

Increasing shear in the warm sector ahead of a cold front helped focus and sustain damaging winds in long-lived linear thunderstorms. After producing large hail and damaging winds in New Hampshire earlier in the afternoon, storms progressed eastward into Maine later in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 0.75 inch hail in Acton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing shear in the warm sector ahead of a cold front helped focus and sustain damaging winds in long-lived linear thunderstorms. After producing large hail and damaging winds in New Hampshire earlier in the afternoon, storms progressed eastward into Maine later in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 0.88 inch hail in North Windham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds occurred across northern and eastern Maine during the evening of the 6th. The thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front crossing the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 200 year old tree was uprooted by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. Other smaller trees were also toppled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred across northeast Aroostook county during the late afternoon and evening of the 11th. The storms developed with an upper disturbance and trof crossing the region.

Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred across northeast Aroostook county during the late afternoon and evening of the 11th. The storms developed with an upper disturbance and trof crossing the region.

Several supercell thunderstorms with very strong damaging winds and large hail moved across northeast Aroostook county during the evening of the 22nd. The storms also produced isolated flash flooding. The storms formed with an upper disturbance crossing the region. The greatest impacts occurred in an area from Ashland...to Caribou...to Fort Fairfield where extensive tree damage and numerous power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility poles and power lines were toppled on South Main Street by wind gusts estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms with very strong damaging winds and large hail moved across northeast Aroostook county during the evening of the 22nd. The storms also produced isolated flash flooding. The storms formed with an upper disturbance crossing the region. The greatest impacts occurred in an area from Ashland...to Caribou...to Fort Fairfield where extensive tree damage and numerous power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An oak tree was split on Lombard Street by wind gusts estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with a pre-frontal trough moved through the forecast area during the afternoon and early evening of July 4th. Ahead of this boundary was very unstable air with high surface dew points and large CAPE values of 2000-2500 J/kg. Wind flow was relatively weak with limited wind shear. These storms produced many reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Ogunquit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough and associated cold front pushed into the region on the afternoon of the 21st. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees between Jackman and the border crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms with very strong damaging winds and large hail moved across northeast Aroostook county during the evening of the 22nd. The storms also produced isolated flash flooding. The storms formed with an upper disturbance crossing the region. The greatest impacts occurred in an area from Ashland...to Caribou...to Fort Fairfield where extensive tree damage and numerous power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with a pre-frontal trough moved through the forecast area during the afternoon and early evening of July 4th. Ahead of this boundary was very unstable air with high surface dew points and large CAPE values of 2000-2500 J/kg. Wind flow was relatively weak with limited wind shear. These storms produced many reports of wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Caratunk.

Low pressure deepened to 997 mb as it moved northeast near Cape Cod on November 23rd, during a period of high astronomical tides. This system produced a prolonged period of northeast winds on the 23rd followed by a northerly flow on the 24th generating a moderate swell. This created a storm surge of close to a foot (0.80 ft). Minor flooding occurred along the wharfs on commercial Street in Portland Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged northeast flow produced by passing low pressure combined with high astronomical high tides to produce minor flooding in Portland on Commercial Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the Carolina coast on the morning of the 11th intensified rapidly as it moved slowly northeast to near Cape Cod by the morning of the 12th, and then to just east of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 13th.  The storm brought heavy snow to the state with some mixed precipitation along the immediate coast.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about 6 inches on the north side of the western mountains to 12 to 18 inches across much of the remainder of the area.  Along the immediate coast, snowfall amounts ranged downward to about 6 inches due to the mixed precipitation.

Low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to Downeast and much of northern Maine. Snow began during the morning of the 2nd...then continued through the early morning hours of the 3rd. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking near the Downeast coast to southern New Brunswick brought heavy snow to northern Maine...with a mixture of snow and ice across interior Downeast areas. Snow began during the afternoon and evening of the 5th...with precipitation continuing into the morning of the 6th. Precipitation remained snow across northern Maine...while warmer air allowed the precipitation to transition to freezing rain and sleet across interior Downeast areas. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 15 inches across northern areas. Snow accumulations across interior Downeast areas generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches along with 1/4 to 1/2 inch of ice. Precipitation transitioned to mostly rain along the Downeast coast...limiting snow and ice accumulations there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 11 inches. Ice accumulations ranged from 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the Carolina coast on the morning of the 11th intensified rapidly as it moved slowly northeast to near Cape Cod by the morning of the 12th, and then to just east of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 13th.  The storm brought heavy snow to the state with some mixed precipitation along the immediate coast.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about 6 inches on the north side of the western mountains to 12 to 18 inches across much of the remainder of the area.  Along the immediate coast, snowfall amounts ranged downward to about 6 inches due to the mixed precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking near the Maine coast brought heavy snow and gusty winds to the region. Snow began during the morning of the 25th...then continued through that evening. Storm total snow accumulations across Downeast areas generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches...with accumulations of 5 to 10 inches across eastcentral areas. Occasional wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph...produced extensive blowing and drifting snow with near whiteout conditions at times. The strongest winds generally occurred near the Downeast coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure lifting north through the Gulf of Maine brought heavy wet snow and strong winds to the region. Snow developed across Downeast areas during the morning of the 1st...then expanded across the rest of the region. The combination of heavy wet snow and strong winds snapped branches and helped bring down powerlines. At the peak of the event...around 10,000 customers were without power for varying amounts of time. Heavy wet snow weighing down branches and powerlines continued to contribute to power outages into the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 15 inches. Winds were sustained at speeds of 15 to 25 mph...with gusts of 30 to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved from the Carolina Coast on the morning of the 5th to near Nova Scotia by the morning of the 6th.  Strong southeasterly flow ahead of the system caused snow to rapidly change to sleet, freezing rain, and rain along the coast and interior.  Farther inland, 6 to 12 inches of snow fell in the mountains and central foothills.

An area of low pressure located over Great Lakes on the morning of the 21st moved east to south of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 22nd.  The low brought a moderate snow to most of western Maine with heavy snow along the midcoast.  In general, snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches across the region with the heaviest amounts in the mid-coast area.

An ice jam developed on the Piscataquis River...near the town of Maxfield...during the afternoon of the 13th. Flooding from the ice jam affected a portion of the North Howland Road near Hardy Brook. Varying water depths ranging from several inches...up to 18 inches...covered the road. Flooding persisted into the morning of the 15th when the ice jam moved and the river was able to undercut the jam allowing flood waters to recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Piscataquis River at the town of Maxfield produced minor flooding along a portion of the North Howland Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed at the confluence of the Mattawamkeag River and Penobscot River at the town of Mattawamkeag during the evening of the 12th. The ice jam persisted overnight with flooding developing during the morning of the 13th. At one point the ice jam was estimated to be around 4 miles long. The flooding affected several main and secondary roads including Route 2 and River Road. Some roads were closed...while other roads were covered with water of varying depths though remained passable. Some families were briefly evacuated along River Road. The ice jam released during the late afternoon of the 13th allowing flood waters to recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam at the confluence of the Mattawamkeag River and Penobscot River caused flooding in the town of Mattawamkeag.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between intense low pressure exiting through the maritimes and high pressure to the west produced high winds across the region. The winds broke branches and toppled or snapped numerous trees which brought down power lines contributing to power outages. Around 12,000 customers lost power for varying amounts of time during this event. The most extensive power outages were reported in a zone extending from southwest Hancock county...northward across Penobscot county...to southern Piscataquis county. Wet soil from recent rains and thawing ground which helped loosen shallow rooted trees contributed to the number of fallen trees. In the town of Etna in Penobscot county a snapped tree fell on an occupied vehicle. No injuries were reported...although the vehicle received major damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds snapped branches and snapped or toppled numerous trees contributing to  numerous power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved off the Carolina coast on the morning of March 31st as a second area of low pressure started to develop in the mid-Atlantic states.  The second low intensified rapidly by the morning of the 1st and was located near Cape Cod.  The low continued to intensify as it moved slowly through the Gulf of Maine to north of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 2nd.  The low brought heavy snow to all of western Maine for April Fools Day.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about 6 to 14 inches across the region with the smaller amounts over the southwest coast.

A stationary front located along the Canadian border provided a focus for heavy rain to develop during the evening of April 26. Up to 3 inches of rain fell in northwest Maine. This caused rivers and streams to rise to minor or moderate flood levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt caused flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35,000 cfs), which crested at 41,800 cfs.

Strong south to southeast winds developed between high pressure east of the maritimes and deep low pressure approaching from the southwest. The strong winds snapped branches and toppled trees which brought down powerlines contributing to numerous power outages. Around 13,000 customers lost power for varying amounts of time. The strong onshore winds...gusting up to 62 mph along the immediate coast...combined with astronomically high tides to produce localized coastal flooding around the time of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong winds snapped numerous trees and branches...with other trees uprooted. The falling trees and branches brought down powerlines which contributed to numerous power outages. A peak wind gust of 60 mph was reported at Brooklin.

Low pressure tracking near the Downeast coast to southern New Brunswick brought heavy snow to northern Maine...with a mixture of snow and ice across interior Downeast areas. Snow began during the afternoon and evening of the 5th...with precipitation continuing into the morning of the 6th. Precipitation remained snow across northern Maine...while warmer air allowed the precipitation to transition to freezing rain and sleet across interior Downeast areas. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 15 inches across northern areas. Snow accumulations across interior Downeast areas generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches along with 1/4 to 1/2 inch of ice. Precipitation transitioned to mostly rain along the Downeast coast...limiting snow and ice accumulations there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across central Maine brought heavy snow to northern portions of the state. Snow began during the early morning hours of the 14th...then continued through the late evening hours. The heaviest snow fell from northwest Aroostook County across northern portions of Somerset and Piscataquis counties where storm total accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 13 inches. Storm total snow accumulations across northeast Aroostook county ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the lower Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 22nd moved northeast to western Pennsylvania by the morning of the 23rd as a secondary low started to develop off the New Jersey coast.  The secondary low intensified as it moved northeast along the coast on the 23rd. Snow developed across the western, central, and northern sections of the state late on the 22nd and became heavier in the early morning hours of the 23rd.  The extreme southwestern part of the state saw mostly rain.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about an inch along the southwestern coast to 6 to 12 inches across much of the remainder of western and mid-coast Maine.  The greatest accumulations occurred in a band from northern York and southern Oxford Counties northeast through Cumberland, Androscoggin and  Kennebec Counties.

Low pressure over the Gulf States on the morning of the 1st moved northeast to the Ohio Valley by the morning of the 2nd as a secondary low developed off the Delmarva Peninsula.  The secondary low then tracked rapidly through the Gulf of Maine on the 2nd and was located in the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 3rd.  The storm brought heavy snow to all of western and central Maine.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches across all of western and central Maine.  The storm followed on the heels of another storm on the 1st that deposited several inches of snow across the region.

An area of low pressure over the lower Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 22nd moved northeast to western Pennsylvania by the morning of the 23rd as a secondary low started to develop off the New Jersey coast.  The secondary low intensified as it moved northeast along the coast on the 23rd. Snow developed across the western, central, and northern sections of the state late on the 22nd and became heavier in the early morning hours of the 23rd.  The extreme southwestern part of the state saw mostly rain.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about an inch along the southwestern coast to 6 to 12 inches across much of the remainder of western and mid-coast Maine.  The greatest accumulations occurred in a band from northern York and southern Oxford Counties northeast through Cumberland, Androscoggin and  Kennebec Counties.

Low pressure over the Gulf States on the morning of the 1st moved northeast to the Ohio Valley by the morning of the 2nd as a secondary low developed off the Delmarva Peninsula.  The secondary low then tracked rapidly through the Gulf of Maine on the 2nd and was located in the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 3rd.  The storm brought heavy snow to all of western and central Maine.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches across all of western and central Maine.  The storm followed on the heels of another storm on the 1st that deposited several inches of snow across the region.

Low pressure tracking near the Downeast coast to southern New Brunswick brought heavy snow to northern Maine...with a mixture of snow and ice across interior Downeast areas. Snow began during the afternoon and evening of the 5th...with precipitation continuing into the morning of the 6th. Precipitation remained snow across northern Maine...while warmer air allowed the precipitation to transition to freezing rain and sleet across interior Downeast areas. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 15 inches across northern areas. Snow accumulations across interior Downeast areas generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches along with 1/4 to 1/2 inch of ice. Precipitation transitioned to mostly rain along the Downeast coast...limiting snow and ice accumulations there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 12 inches. Ice accumulations were 1/4 of an inch or less in spots.

An area of low pressure formed off Cape Hatteras, NC on the morning of the 18th and intensified as it moved northeast to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 19th.  The storm brought moderate to heavy snow to the area with the precipitation in southern and coastal areas changing to sleet and freezing rain.  Along the immediate coast, the precipitation changed to rain as temperatures warmed above freezing.  Snowfall amounts across the area generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches, with lesser amounts near the coast.

An ice jam on the Penobscot River caused flooding to develop during the morning of the 14th near the town of Chester. This ice jam was caused by ice from the jam which had released upriver from near Mattawamkeag the afternoon before. The flooding affected the Butterfield Ridge Road and the North Main Road...with portions of both roads closed. Low lying areas along the Penobscot River in the vicinity of the ice jam were also flooded. Several homes were also affected by the flooding. The ice jam released during the evening of the 17th allowing the flood waters to recede.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Penobscot River near the town of Chester produced flooding which closed portions of Butterfield Ridge Road and North Main Road.

Low pressure deepened to 985 mb in the Gulf of Maine during a period of high astronomical tides.  This system produced north to northwest winds along the Maine coast with very little wave action.  Nevertheless, a storm surge of over a foot (1.58 feet) occurred.  Minor flooding occurred along the wharfs on Commercial Street in Portland, Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Gulf of Maine, combined with an astronomical high tide, produced coastal flooding along the wharfs on Commercial Street in Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving east from Western New York on the morning of the 28th brought a mix of precipitation to Western Maine.  In the mountains, the storm brought 5 to 8 inches of snow while locations south of the mountains saw a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet and snow with light to moderate accumulations.

An area of low pressure over the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 25th moved rapidly east during the day to the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 26th.  The low brought 8 to 12 inches of snow to the region with locally higher amounts.  Along coastal York County and the extreme southern portions of Cumberland, Sagadahoc, Lincoln and Knox Counties, the snow mixed with or changed to rain during the day, keeping accumulations lower in those areas.

On Saturday morning, March 5th, a frontal system was located over the Ohio and Mississippi River Valleys.  Ahead of the front, south to southwest winds ushered in some very mild air.  Temperatures across most of southern and central Maine warmed into the lower 50s during the day and remained well above freezing Saturday night.     By Sunday morning, March 6th, the front had moved east to the Appalachians and the strong southerly flow ahead of the system continued to bring mild temperatures and tropical moisture northeast from the Gulf of Mexico and southern Atlantic Ocean.   Temperatures Sunday climbed into the upper 40s to middle 50s across the southern part of the state.   An area of low pressure began to develop over the southeastern states Sunday and then moved northeast along the frontal boundary Sunday night and Monday as it intensified rapidly.  Light rain, associated with the approaching cold front began to fall Sunday morning.  The precipitation became heavy by late afternoon as the intensifying area of low pressure approached Maine from the south.  At the same time, colder air started funneling into the state from the north and changed the rain to snow across the Western Mountains during the afternoon.   Across the state, heavy snow, freezing rain and rain continued through the night and finally ended Monday afternoon, March 7th.  In addition to the precipitation, strong and gusty winds developed across the region Sunday night and continued Monday.  ||Across Western Mountains, snowfall accumulations from the storm ranged from 13 to 23 inches.  ||Rainfall amounts across the southern part of the state were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range, with locally higher amounts.  In addition, melting snow, due to the mild temperatures, contributed to the runoff from the storm.  The rainfall and melting snow caused river levels to rise which caused river ice to break and move downstream.

In addition to the precipitation, strong and gusty winds developed across the region Sunday night and continued Monday.  ||Across Western Mountains, snowfall accumulations from the storm ranged from 13 to 23 inches.  ||Rainfall amounts across the southern part of the state were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range, with locally higher amounts.  In addition, melting snow, due to the mild temperatures, contributed to the runoff from the storm.  The rainfall and melting snow caused river levels to rise which caused river ice to break and move downstream.

Low pressure tracking along a stalled frontal boundary across Downeast Maine brought abundant moisture to the region.  Across northern portions of the region rain transitioned to snow during the evening of the 6th with colder air in the wake of the front. Across central portions of the region...just north of the stalled front...low levels cooled while the low drew warmer air aloft over the frontal boundary. This resulted in snow and sleet transitioning to freezing rain. Snow north...with mostly freezing rain across central portions of the region...then continued through the afternoon of the 7th. Storm total snow accumulations of 15 to 20 inches were common across northern portions of Aroostook...Piscataquis and Somerset counties...with totals of around 24 inches across extreme northwest areas. Storm total snow accumulations in a zone from southeast Aroostook county...across northern Penobscot county...to central and southern Piscataquis county varied significantly. Snow accumulations across northern portions of this zone generally ranged from 8 to 14 inches...with 2 to 6 inches across the remainder of the region where a greater portion of the precipitation fell in the form of sleet and freezing rain. Ice accumulations of 1/4 to 3/4 of an inch were common...with localized totals of around 1 inch. South of the frontal boundary...across Downeast Maine...enough warm air was drawn into the storm to allow most of the precipitation to fall in the form of rain.||The combination of heavy wet snow and significant ice accumulation snapped branches...toppled and weighed down many trees. The falling trees and branches...along with ice build up on wires...brought down numerous power lines contributing to power outages. The most numerous power outages occurred in a zone from southern Piscataquis county....across central and northern Penobscot county...to southeast Aroostook county. Thousands of customers lost power for varying amounts of time in this zone...some without power for over 24 hours. Fallen trees and power lines also blocked roads in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 15 to 20 inches across eastern areas with 20 to 25 inches across western areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Saturday morning, March 5th, a frontal system was located over the Ohio and Mississippi River Valleys.  Ahead of the front, south to southwest winds ushered in some very mild air.  Temperatures across most of southern and central Maine warmed into the lower 50s during the day and remained well above freezing Saturday night.     By Sunday morning, March 6th, the front had moved east to the Appalachians and the strong southerly flow ahead of the system continued to bring mild temperatures and tropical moisture northeast from the Gulf of Mexico and southern Atlantic Ocean.   Temperatures Sunday climbed into the upper 40s to middle 50s across the southern part of the state.   An area of low pressure began to develop over the southeastern states Sunday and then moved northeast along the frontal boundary Sunday night and Monday as it intensified rapidly.  Light rain, associated with the approaching cold front began to fall Sunday morning.  The precipitation became heavy by late afternoon as the intensifying area of low pressure approached Maine from the south.  At the same time, colder air started funneling into the state from the north and changed the rain to snow across the Western Mountains during the afternoon.   Across the state, heavy snow, freezing rain and rain continued through the night and finally ended Monday afternoon, March 7th.  In addition to the precipitation, strong and gusty winds developed across the region Sunday night and continued Monday.  ||Across Western Mountains, snowfall accumulations from the storm ranged from 13 to 23 inches.  ||Rainfall amounts across the southern part of the state were generally in the 1 to 3 inch range, with locally higher amounts.  In addition, melting snow, due to the mild temperatures, contributed to the runoff from the storm.  The rainfall and melting snow caused river levels to rise which caused river ice to break and move downstream.

Low pressure developed in southern New England and moved northeast along the coast of Maine. Precipitation ranged from around 1 inch or less in the mountains to 2 or more inches along the coast. The runoff was enhanced by melting snow. Minor flooding occurred on area rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt caused flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35,000 cfs), which crested at 38,000 cfs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt caused minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Augusta (flood stage 12.0 ft), which crested at 12.2 ft.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt caused flooding on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook (flood stage 15.0 ft), which crested at 17.5 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across central Maine brought heavy snow to northern portions of the state. Snow began during the early morning hours of the 14th...then continued through the late evening hours. The heaviest snow fell from northwest Aroostook County across northern portions of Somerset and Piscataquis counties where storm total accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 13 inches. Storm total snow accumulations across northeast Aroostook county ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a strong cold front kicked off a morning round of showers and thunderstorms. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in southwest Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Gorham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow and gusty winds from Downeast areas to northern portions of Piscataquis and Penobscot counties along with southeast Aroostook county. Snow began during the morning of the 21st then continued through the late evening hours. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches with local totals to around 18 inches across much of the region. However...a persistent snow band produced totals of up to 20 to 22 inches in a narrow ribbon across southeast Aroostook county. Wind gusts of around 30 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow across the region...with near whiteout conditions at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 11 inches.  Wind gusts to around 30 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow along with reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia produced heavy snow and gusty winds across Downeast and eastcentral Maine. The snow began during the morning of the 12th and continued into the early morning hours of the 13th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches across eastcentral areas...to 10 to 14 inches Downeast. Occasional wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph produced near blizzard conditions at times. The combination of heavy snow and gusty winds contributed to power outages across portions of Downeast Maine...where around 3000 customers lost power in Washington and Hancock counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 14 inches. Occasional wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow with near whiteout conditions at times. The combination of heavy snow and winds brought down branches and power lines contributing to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking rapidly along the Maine coast to the maritimes brought heavy snow to portions of northeast Maine. Snow began during the early morning hours of the 8th then continued through mid morning. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 9 inches across northeast Aroostook county with 4 to 7 inches across southeast Aroostook county. Lesser snow accumulations occurred across northwest Maine. The lack of cold air limited snow accumulations further south across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet developed in advance of intensifying low pressure crossing Maine. Onshore south to southeast winds sustained at speeds of 30 to 40 mph...with gusts approaching 70 mph...occurred along coastal Washington county during the morning of the 28th. The winds toppled trees...snapped branches and brought down power lines which contributed to power outages. Around 1600 customers lost power across coastal Washington county. A peak wind gust of 69 mph was reported at Lubec...with a peak gust of 60 mph at Eastport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 69 mph was reported at Lubec...with a peak gust of 60 mph at Eastport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow and gusty winds from Downeast areas to northern portions of Piscataquis and Penobscot counties along with southeast Aroostook county. Snow began during the morning of the 21st then continued through the late evening hours. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches with local totals to around 18 inches across much of the region. However...a persistent snow band produced totals of up to 20 to 22 inches in a narrow ribbon across southeast Aroostook county. Wind gusts of around 30 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow across the region...with near whiteout conditions at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 11 to 17 inches. Wind gusts to around 30 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow along with reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking near the Downeast coast to southern New Brunswick brought heavy snow to northern Maine...with a mixture of snow and ice across interior Downeast areas. Snow began during the afternoon and evening of the 5th...with precipitation continuing into the morning of the 6th. Precipitation remained snow across northern Maine...while warmer air allowed the precipitation to transition to freezing rain and sleet across interior Downeast areas. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 15 inches across northern areas. Snow accumulations across interior Downeast areas generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches along with 1/4 to 1/2 inch of ice. Precipitation transitioned to mostly rain along the Downeast coast...limiting snow and ice accumulations there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 9 inches. Ice accumulations were less than 1/4 of an inch in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking near the Downeast coast to southern New Brunswick brought heavy snow to northern Maine...with a mixture of snow and ice across interior Downeast areas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough was digging into New England on the afternoon of July 26th with an associated cold front approaching the region. Instability was marginal with CAPE's of 1000 j/kg or less generally pooled ahead of the front. However... dynamics associated with the shortwave and moderate to strong low level shear produced numerous severe cells with large hail and damaging winds. In addition ...heavy rain with some of these storms resulted in flash flooding and road washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Gully Oven Road in Milton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across Aroostook County along a nearly stationary frontal boundary. The storms developed during the late afternoon and continued into the evening. The storms primarily produced damaging winds...though two weak tornadoes also occurred. Heavy rain from training thunderstorms produced localized flash flooding in the Fort Kent area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were toppled by wind gusts estimated at 70 mph.

A surface cold front tracking southeast, combined with good height falls aloft and jet exit region, set off a broken line of strong storms and supercells across the county warning area. Some storms formed on or were enhanced by the sea breeze boundary. 1 inch hail was common with several storms, especially just south of Portland, and in the Augusta area. A few storms also produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Oxford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across Aroostook County along a nearly stationary frontal boundary. The storms developed during the late afternoon and continued into the evening. The storms primarily produced damaging winds...though two weak tornadoes also occurred. Heavy rain from training thunderstorms produced localized flash flooding in the Fort Kent area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado snapped or uprooted numerous trees along an intermittent path. The tornado initially touched down around 1 mile east-southeast of Little Madawaska Lake. The tornado was embedded within an area of damaging straight line winds...intermittently touching down along its path. The final touch down point was around 4 miles west-northwest of Loring Air Force Base. Most of the damage along the path was uprooted or snapped trees. However...the tornado destroyed an older barn (DI 1, DOD 5) and damaged several farm outbuildings on the Brissette Road. Several other outbuildings were damaged or destroyed at various points along the path. Several homes (DI 2, DOD 2) received damage to shingles and siding. Damage along the path was due to a combination of straight line winds and the intermittent tornado touchdowns.

Water depths of up to 18 to 24 inches were reported on Sweden Street at the height of the flash flooding. Flash flooding damaged portions of River Road. Other roads and culverts were washed out or damaged due to the flooding. Fields were also significantly damaged and eroded in spots due to the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred with a squall line crossing the region in advance of a cold front...with a weak tornado also reported. Heavy rains produced localized flash flooding around Caribou and Fort Fairfield in northeast Aroostook County. The squall line crossed the region from the mid afternoon into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were toppled by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough was digging into New England on the afternoon of July 26th with an associated cold front approaching the region. Instability was marginal with CAPE's of 1000 j/kg or less generally pooled ahead of the front. However... dynamics associated with the shortwave and moderate to strong low level shear produced numerous severe cells with large hail and damaging winds. In addition ...heavy rain with some of these storms resulted in flash flooding and road washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced an estimated gust of 60 mph in Carthage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia brought the first significant snow of the season to portions of Downeast Maine...east-central Maine and the central Maine highlands. Snow began during the night of the 29th...then persisted through the morning of the 30th.|The heaviest snow accumulations occurred in a line from southern Piscataquis county...across central Penobscot county to northern Washington county and portions of southeast Aroostook county. Accumulations in this band generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches...with localized totals to around 10 inches. Accumulations across Downeast Maine varied significantly...ranging from less than an inch up to 7 inches.|Temperatures remained above freezing through much of the storm...leading to a heavy wet snow which weighed down branches and power lines. The heavy wet snow combined with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph along the coast and 30 to 40 mph inland to snap branches...topple trees and bring down power lines. Thousands of customers lost power for varying amounts of time during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 6 inches. Heavy wet snow combined with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph to snap branches and bring down trees and power lines which contributed to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across the southern Gulf of Maine during the 23rd brought heavy snow to east-central and Downeast Maine through the morning and afternoon. Snow accumulations along the immediate coast ranged from an inch or less up to 3 inches where mostly rain...or mixed rain and snow...occurred during the storm. However...only several miles inland from the coast and across higher terrain areas along the Downeast coast precipitation remained snow which accumulated 5 to 12 inches. Storm total snow accumulations of 7 to 10 inches were common across interior Downeast and east-central areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from less than an inch along the immediate coast...up to 11 inches just inland from the coast and across higher terrain areas. Heavy wet snow on branches and power lines contributed to power outages with around 1500 customers losing power at various times during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed off the coast of North Carolina Saturday morning, October 29, then moved north to just east of the Delmarva Peninsula by Saturday evening, and through the Gulf of Maine Saturday night.  The storm brought a heavy, wet snow across Western Maine from the coast to the foothills.  Snow began to fall across southwestern Maine late Saturday afternoon, became heavy during the night, and ended Sunday morning.  The snow was mixed with rain along the immediate coast.  The combination of the heavy wet snow and leaves still on the trees caused numerous trees and branches to snap and fall, causing widespread power outages.  About 207,000 customers lost power during the storm, mostly across the southwestern part of the state.  Some customers were without electrical service for 3 days.||Snowfall amounts were quite variable across the state with southern areas and the higher terrain receiving the most snow, and in some cases, record snowfall for late October.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches along the immediate coast to 10 to 18 inches across the foothills of Western Maine.   In general, the mountains  received 3 to 6 inches of snow.

A warm front pushed northward through the region early on the 4th bringing warm and humid air into much of the region. An upper trough approaching from the west provided dynamic forcing and increasing wind shear during the afternoon in northwest portions of Maine. Several severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon with damaging winds the main feature, although some hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires in West Forks.

A weak south to southeasterly flow across New England coincided with the highest astronomical tides of the month and caused minor coastal flooding.  There was very little wave action with this system which helped mitigate any problems.  Nevertheless, a storm surge of around a half foot (0.61 feet) occurred.  Minor flooding occurred along the wharfs on Commercial Street in Portland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An astronomical high tide produced minor coastal flooding in Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front caused 2 to 4 inches of rain over a 2-day period resulting in minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front caused 2 to 4 inches of rain over a 2 day period resulting in minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan.  The River crested at 36,366 cfs.  Flood flow is 35,000 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of surface low pressure moving into New England spawned areas of showers and thunderstorms. One isolated storm produced some wind damage in mid coast Maine. Another storm produced a gust of 43 mph at a buoy offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on power lines in Phippsburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about 2 miles northwest of the town of Bryant Pond.  The tornado touched down along Gore Road on the southwest shore of North Pond and moved out over North Pond before dissipating.  The tornado snapped or uprooted numerous trees.  Maximum winds were estimated at 85 to 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed out ahead of a cold front during the afternoon of June 1 and produced mostly 1  to 2.75 inch hail across the mountains and foothills of Maine with 1 inch hail reported in some interior areas.  In addition, tornadoes developed  in Oxford and Somerset Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on June 1 in Somerset County.  The tornado touched down near School Street in the town of New Portland where minor damage was observed.  From there, the tornado traveled east about 8.4 miles through the town of Embden to the Kennebec River where it lifted off the ground.  The tornado crossed Embden Pond, Sand Pond, and Fahi Pond in Embden.  Along its path, the tornado snapped or uprooted hundreds of trees and damaged  several buildings and at least one vehicle.  The most significant damage was observed near Embden Pond Road where winds were estimated to be between 90 an 100 mph.  The maximum path width was estimated to be about 200 yards near Embden Pond Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating ahead of an approaching cold front generated moderate to strong instability. Instability increased as the front neared and a large line formed ahead of the front... with numerous smaller cells forming out in front of the main line. Most severe reports were almost exclusively associated with wind damage as numerous trees and power lines were downed throughout the forecast area.  In addition, lightning struck the lodge at Mt. Abram Ski Resort and ignited a fire that heavily damaged the lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail and strong gusty winds near North Windham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms with very strong damaging winds and large hail moved across northeast Aroostook county during the evening of the 22nd. The storms also produced isolated flash flooding. The storms formed with an upper disturbance crossing the region. The greatest impacts occurred in an area from Ashland...to Caribou...to Fort Fairfield where extensive tree damage and numerous power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled on West Road...with numerous trees toppled and snapped on Hedman Road...by wind gusts estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Irene made a final landfall around New York City as a Category 1 hurricane then weakened to a tropical storm while lifting northeast across New England. The storm then transitioned to a post-tropical cyclone across northwest Maine during the night of the 28th.|Tropical Storm Irene brought heavy rains and gusty winds to northern and eastern Maine through the afternoon into the night of the 28th. Sustained winds generally ranged from 20 to 30 mph...locally up to around 35 mph. A peak wind gust of 63 mph was reported at Sedgwick during the late afternoon. Wind gusts across northern and eastern Maine generally ranged from 30 to 40 mph...with localized gusts of 50 to 60 mph with convective elements. The winds toppled trees and power lines...with the most extensive damage from Piscataquis county southward across Downeast Maine. Around 30,000 customers lost power across these areas...with scattered power outages across northern Maine.|Rainfall totals across northern and eastern Maine generally ranged from 1.50 to 3.00 inches...with localized totals of 4.00 to 5.00 inches. Isolated totals approaching 10.00 inches were reported around Baxter State Park where upslope into the mountains enhanced rainfall. No significant river or stream flooding was reported across the region. However...flash flooding in Baxter State Park did wash out numerous roads.|The minimum surface pressure reported during the storm was around 985 mb. Storm surge was not a factor during the storm...with no tornadoes reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph were common with Tropical Storm Irene...with some gusts in excess of 50 mph. The winds brought down many trees and power lines...leading to numerous power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Irene moving north through western New England produced very heavy rain across western Maine. Flash flooding in the northwestern part of the state resulted in over a million dollars in damages to roads and bridges in Oxford and Franklin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from Tropical Storm Irene resulted in flash flooding in Sandy River Plantation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms in northwest Maine. Large hail was the main feature with these storms although there were some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Wyman Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms in northwest Maine. Large hail was the main feature with these storms although there were some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 2.5 inch hail near Bingham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms in northwest Maine. Large hail was the main feature with these storms although there were some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Bingham.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Bingham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms in northwest Maine. Large hail was the main feature with these storms although there were some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced .88 inch hail near Middledam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms in northwest Maine. Large hail was the main feature with these storms although there were some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail at Silver Lake Campground near Roxbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms in northwest Maine. Large hail was the main feature with these storms although there were some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree across Route 4 near Rangely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms in northwest Maine. Large hail was the main feature with these storms although there were some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large trees on Brighton Road in Solon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was again the focus for severe thunderstorms in northwest Maine. Large hail was the main feature with these storms although there were some reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced estimated gusts of 50 to 60 mph breaking boats free from the dock near South Arm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northern Aroostook County in advance of a cold front. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail near Ashland in northeast Aroostook County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough set up over the region with rich low level moisture in place. This provided the focus for strong afternoon convection beginning to the southwest of the forecast area and moving northeast along the boundary into western Maine. The storms trained repeatedly along the boundary across northern Oxford...northern Franklin...and northern Somerset counties, producing heavy rain and one report of large hail. Some areas received between 2-4 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Andover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred across northeast Aroostook county during the late afternoon and evening of the 11th. The storms developed with an upper disturbance and trof crossing the region.

Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail occurred across northern Aroostook county during the late afternoon of the 12th. Isolated flash flooding also occurred across portions of the Saint John Valley. The storms developed along a slow moving frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were toppled and snapped along Route 161 by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph. Portions of Route 161 were blocked due to fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail occurred across northern Aroostook county during the late afternoon of the 12th. Isolated flash flooding also occurred across portions of the Saint John Valley. The storms developed along a slow moving frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled on Route 11 by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy rain lifting across northern Maine produced localized flooding in Eagle Lake during the evening of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains produced localized rainfall totals of 3.00 to 5.00 inches in several hours. The heavy rains led to flooding around Eagle Lake. Culverts overflowed and were damaged due to the flooding. The Perley Brook overflowed onto Aroostook Road. Water depths up to 6 inches were reported on Albert Street. Several basements were flooded...with one home receiving more significant flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave digging into northern New England produced plunging mid and upper level temperatures resulting in steep lapse rates across the forecast area. Freezing levels dropping to around ten thousand feet set the stage for large hail with some of the stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.50 inch hail in Troy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds occurred with a squall line crossing the region in advance of a cold front...with a weak tornado also reported. Heavy rains produced localized flash flooding around Caribou and Fort Fairfield in northeast Aroostook County. The squall line crossed the region from the mid afternoon into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled along Route 1A between Fort Fairfield and Limestone by wind gusts estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong heating ahead of an approaching cold front generated moderate to strong instability. Instability increased as the front neared and a large line formed ahead of the front... with numerous smaller cells forming out in front of the main line. Most severe reports were almost exclusively associated with wind damage as numerous trees and power lines were downed throughout the forecast area.  In addition, lightning struck the lodge at Mt. Abram Ski Resort and ignited a fire that heavily damaged the lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced numerous reports of trees down in Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front crossing the region producing large hail and damaging winds. The initial thunderstorms formed across northern and northwest Maine during the afternoon...reaching interior Downeast areas during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled on to a road in New Canada by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across Aroostook County along a nearly stationary frontal boundary. The storms developed during the late afternoon and continued into the evening. The storms primarily produced damaging winds...though two weak tornadoes also occurred. Heavy rain from training thunderstorms produced localized flash flooding in the Fort Kent area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado briefly touched down damaging trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough and associated cold front pushed into the region on the afternoon of the 21st. Good low level moisture and instability combined with an impressive wind field aloft to produce numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. All reports of damage were due to strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on a house on Oak Street in Lebanon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms with very strong damaging winds and large hail moved across northeast Aroostook county during the evening of the 22nd. The storms also produced isolated flash flooding. The storms formed with an upper disturbance crossing the region. The greatest impacts occurred in an area from Ashland...to Caribou...to Fort Fairfield where extensive tree damage and numerous power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trailer on the Grimes Road was lifted off its foundation by wind gusts estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds developed during the afternoon of the 4th in advance of a cold front crossing the region. Isolated flash flooding also occurred in the Caribou area. The thunderstorms continued into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled on to power lines on the Bondeson Road by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.
